coredata.dc:description: The remarkable optical properties of carbon dots (CDs) render them highly promising as a versatile group of carbon-based nanomaterials. Integrating hydrothermally synthesized CDs into zinc aluminate doped with Pr3+ ions (ZnAl2O4:Pr3+) nanophosphors (ZAO:Pr3+ NPs) fabricated via the solution combustion (SC) technique holds great potential. The aim of synthesizing these nanocrystals (NCs) is to explore their potential uses in optical thermometry and anti-counterfeiting (AC) measures. The synthesized nanoparticles (NPs) and nanocomposites (NCs) underwent characterization using X-ray diffraction (XRD), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and photoluminescence (PL) spectra to verify their phase, morphology, particle size, oxidation state, and chemical composition. When excited at 447 nm, the Pr3+ doped ZAO: NPs exhibited an orange-red emission band at 614 nm. A remarkable enhancement in PL intensity, by a factor of 39.02 folds, is noted upon embedding CDs into the ZAO:Pr3+ NPs (CDs@ ZAO:Pr3+). The enhanced PL intensity can be ascribed to the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. Even at a temperature of 423 K, the NPs retains 95.4% of its emission intensity compared to that at room temperature, showcasing exceptional thermal stability. The 5 wt% CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs demonstrate a high colour purity (CP) of 98.3%. Moreover, these NCs hold promise for optical thermometry applications across a broad temperature range spanning from 303 to 463 K. Utilizing the exceptional ZAO:1Pr3+ NPs and 5 wt% CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs, two representative white light emitting diodes (w-LEDs) have been successfully developed, boasting satisfactory luminous efficacy and colour-rendering index (CRI). This underscores their potential for high-performance w-LED applications. Simultaneously, a highly sensitive, non-contact optical thermometer has been engineered, featuring maximum relative sensitivities of approximately 44.51×10−4 K −1 and 75.48×10−4 K −1 at the emission intensities corresponding to 673, 693, 712 and 735 nm, respectively. We have developed a simple brush mode technique for creating a variety of patterns using the manufactured AC security ink. The latent fingerprints (LFPs) visualized using 5 wt% CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs exhibit excellent resolution and contrast, enabling the easy identification of fingerprint characteristics from levels I-III. Employing deep learning utilizing the YOLOv8x algorithm, fluorescence images of the revealed LFPs demonstrate remarkable alignment with standard controls, suggesting a high degree of similarity. In addition, the fabricated white light emitting diode (w-LED) boasts a favorable colour rendering index (Ra=87) alongside Commission International de L′Eclairage (CIE) coordinates (0.617, 0.376). Consequently, the inclusion of 5wt% CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+NPs showcases remarkable luminescent attributes and holds promising prospects across various applications.

Preparation of security ink and creating AC pattern using 5Wt%CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The possibility of F\u00F6rster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) between CDs and Pr3+ ions is one explanation for this phenomenon. In theory, FRET needs three basic requirements: The donor and acceptor molecules must, first and foremost, be near to one another (usually within 1\u201310 nm). This need is supported by the TEM image of the CDs@ ZAO:1Pr3+ composite (Fig. 6), which shows the CDs firmly attached to the ZAO:Pr3+ surface. Second, the acceptors (ZAO:Pr3+) excitation spectrum and the donor's (CDs) emission spectrum ought to overlap. This energy transfer can be observed in the spectral region shown in Fig. 9h. Effective energy transfer is facilitated by the large spectral overlap that can be achieved between the PL of CDs and the PLE of phosphors thanks to the broad PL spectra of CDs [66\u201369]. A higher spectrum overlap is seen in the CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+composite, which lends more credence to the possibility of FRET. As a result, ZAO:Pr3+can receive the excitation energy from CDs with greater effectiveness. The donor and acceptor transition dipole orientations also normally have to be parallel [70,71]. The transition dipole moments of donor and acceptors interact D-d to enhance energy transfer, which is made possible by FRET. Once the donor (CDs) absorbs light and becomes excited, it can either radiatively relax through fluorescence or non-radiatively transfer energy to the acceptor (Pr3+). The electrons in Pr3+ transition from the 3P0 to different components of the excited 3f energy level when CDs@ZAO:Pr3+ are excited at a wavelength of 447 nm, which describes the mechanism of luminescence enhancement. On the other hand, electrons in CDs transition, with the help of absorbed photon energy, from the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) to the Lowest Un-occupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) (Fig. 9i). When compared to ZAO:Pr3+ phosphors, the emission intensity of 5wt% CDs@ZAO:Pr3+ composite shows an increase of about 39.02 times (Fig. 9f). The shift in FWHM values points to a reduction in positive and negative defects as a result of CDs inclusion, producing an oxidation/reduction effect, which is responsible for the increased crystallinity in the 5wt% CDs@ZAO:Pr3+ composite. Additionally, the 5wt% CDs@ZAO:Pr3+ composite show a wide 400\u2013500 nm excitation spectrum with a major peak at 447 nm, which corresponds to the emission range (425\u2013600 nm) of common LED chips. This property increases the range of possible uses for luminous materials while providing improved performance and adaptability [102]. A comparative analysis of improved PL in various CDs comprised from different research is shown in Table 5 [72\u201377].The author (H. N.) acknowledges VGST, Govt. of Karnataka, India [VGST/KFIST-4/GRD-489] for sanction of the project. The authors (K. ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The remarkable optical properties of carbon dots (CDs) render them highly promising as a versatile group of carbon-based nanomaterials. Integrating hydrothermally synthesized CDs into zinc aluminate doped with Pr3+ ions (ZnAl2O4:Pr3+) nanophosphors (ZAO:Pr3+ NPs) fabricated via the solution combustion (SC) technique holds great potential. The aim of synthesizing these nanocrystals (NCs) is to explore their potential uses in optical thermometry and anti-counterfeiting (AC) measures. The synthesized nanoparticles (NPs) and nanocomposites (NCs) underwent characterization using X-ray diffraction (XRD), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and photoluminescence (PL) spectra to verify their phase, morphology, particle size, oxidation state, and chemical composition. When excited at 447 nm, the Pr3+ doped ZAO: NPs exhibited an orange-red emission band at 614 nm. A remarkable enhancement in PL intensity, by a factor of 39.02 folds, is noted upon embedding CDs into the ZAO:Pr3+ NPs (CDs@ ZAO:Pr3+). The enhanced PL intensity can be ascribed to the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. Even at a temperature of 423 K, the NPs retains 95.4% of its emission intensity compared to that at room temperature, showcasing exceptional thermal stability. The 5 wt% CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs demonstrate a high colour purity (CP) of 98.3%. Moreover, these NCs hold promise for optical thermometry applications across a broad temperature range spanning from 303 to 463 K. Utilizing the exceptional ZAO:1Pr3+ NPs and 5 wt% CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs, two representative white light emitting diodes (w-LEDs) have been successfully developed, boasting satisfactory luminous efficacy and colour-rendering index (CRI). This underscores their potential for high-performance w-LED applications. Simultaneously, a highly sensitive, non-contact optical thermometer has been engineered, featuring maximum relative sensitivities of approximately 44.51×10−4 K −1 and 75.48×10−4 K −1 at the emission intensities corresponding to 673, 693, 712 and 735 nm, respectively. We have developed a simple brush mode technique for creating a variety of patterns using the manufactured AC security ink. The latent fingerprints (LFPs) visualized using 5 wt% CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs exhibit excellent resolution and contrast, enabling the easy identification of fingerprint characteristics from levels I-III. Employing deep learning utilizing the YOLOv8x algorithm, fluorescence images of the revealed LFPs demonstrate remarkable alignment with standard controls, suggesting a high degree of similarity. In addition, the fabricated white light emitting diode (w-LED) boasts a favorable colour rendering index (Ra=87) alongside Commission International de L′Eclairage (CIE) coordinates (0.617, 0.376). Consequently, the inclusion of 5wt% CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+NPs showcases remarkable luminescent attributes and holds promising prospects across various applications.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Material science provides the fundamental basis for both science and technology, with technological advancements hinging on the understanding and manipulation of the chemical and physical properties of materials. Different technologies rely on particular synthesized materials because the characteristics of these materials are greatly shaped by the method of synthesis utilized [1–3]. Different phosphor substances are created through varying parameters and methods of synthesis [4-6]. Inorganic phosphors synthesized exhibit distinct luminescent properties and valuable characteristics. Several commonly utilized substances, including tungstate, borate, oxynitride, phosphate, oxide, silicate, carbonate, aluminates, nitride, selenide, halide, oxyhalide, and sulfide, among others, serve as host materials for synthesizing phosphors. Variations from the perfect crystal structure are commonly encountered in both naturally occurring minerals and artificially synthesized (inorganic) crystalline substances. This could involve the creation of defects and antisite mixing, which have the potential to enhance certain material properties, such as conductivity, thus advancing their practical applications [7,8].

Oxide spinels (AB2O4) constitute a distinctive category of ceramics known for their rich structural (dis)ordering. An example of such a spinel is gahnite, also known as zinc aluminate spinel (ZnAl2O4), which has garnered considerable interest due to its low surface acidity, exceptional thermal/mechanical stability, and rapid diffusivity. Moreover, the selection of the host matrix significantly influences the improvement of luminescent properties. Spinel-structured zinc aluminate (ZnAl2O4), commonly referred to as Gahnite, belongs to the family of metal aluminates and stands out as a prominent member among inorganic materials. It attracts significant research attention for its applications in ceramics, electronics, and catalysis [9-13]. In the ZnAl2O4 (ZAO) structure, zinc cations are situated in tetrahedral (ZnO4) coordination, whereas aluminum cations occupy octahedral (AlO6) coordinated sites [14]. Zinc aluminate has garnered significant attention because of its exceptional thermal and chemical stability, high quantum yields, and other favorable characteristics. Because of its exceptional thermal and chemical stability, hydrophobic properties, photocatalytic activity and transparency, Gahnite finds application in diverse fields such as optical coatings, high-temperature materials, dielectrics and catalysts [15,16]. Various chemical methods have been employed in endeavours to synthesize ZAO phosphors [17-19]. Among the different synthesis approaches, the combustion method is straightforward, cost-effective, and requires less time. Recently, Gahnite has emerged as an intriguing host material for designing a variety of optoelectronic components. Several studies documented in the literature investigate the introduction of different transition metal (TM) ions (like Co2+ and Mn2+) and rare-earth (RE) ions (Eu3+, Dy3+, Tb3+) into Gahnite, making it an exceptional phosphor material suitable for a wide range of optical applications [20-22].

Owing to its wide range of temperature-dependent emission, lanthanide ions are advantageous as emission centers [23]. Praseodymium ion (Pr3+) is one of the most well-known lanthanide luminescence probes in recent times [24]. It exhibits red and green-blue emissions, which can increase the thermometer's operational temperature range and enhance its accuracy and thermal sensitivity. Several Pr3+doped phosphors have demonstrated outstanding sensing capability and a richness of 4f-4f transition lines in the visible band. Cross relaxation (CR) between 3P0 and 1D2, the thermal connection of the 3P1 and 3P0 levels, and the variable emissions from the 3P0, 1 and 1D2 excited states are the causes of this [25]. Moreover, the emission bands of Pr3+ doped phosphors extend from the ultraviolet to the infrared owing to the closely spaced energy levels of the Pr3+ ion and the differing magnitudes of energy gaps [26]. Furthermore, Pr3+ ions are less expensive than some other rare earth ions, which makes it possible to produce Pr3+ doped phosphors on a wide scale for less money. Although they have already shown promise for noncontact temperature monitoring, their main drawback is the comparatively modest emission intensity brought on by thermal quenching at high temperatures.

The total emission intensity is lowered by the thermal quenching effect. Still, a steady output for the thermometer will be aided by a strong enough light signal. Enabling luminous changes, such fluorescence intensity ratio (FIR), in self-referencing luminescent thermometers by creating energy transfer modes, like cross relaxation (CR), at different temperatures is crucial to enabling stable temperature measurement. This method guarantees that the optical signal for temperature measurement is still observable and helps to avoid unduly weakening the total emission intensity. To address the issue of low signal intensity, anti-thermal quenching Pr3+ doped phosphors must be developed for thermometer applications.

Moreover, the integration of CDs into NPs has demonstrated effective enhancement of their luminous efficiency. Composites of CDs@ZnGa2O4 displayed notably heightened PL intensity due to elevated disorder and vacancy concentrations [27]. Further exploration into the enhancement of phosphor luminescence through the incorporation of CDs is conducted by investigating the energy transfer mechanism. The luminescence of CDs@YF3:Eu3+ NCs is notably enhanced by the energy transfer from CDs to Eu3+ [28]. Moreover, ZBH:Tm3+/yNCDs composites displayed improved luminescence, attributed to the energy transfer between nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) and Tm3+ ions [29]. Compare to YBO3:Eu3+, CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ shows high PL intensity which is attributed to the FRET mechanism between Eu3+ and CDs [30]. CDs have been incorporated into MAl2O4 based phosphors, such as ZAO, to achieve specific luminescence characteristics.

Given the escalating rate of counterfeiting in valuable commodities like currency, documents, and consumer goods, there is an immediate demand for efficient AC techniques [31,32]. To protect crucial documents and products, a variety of strategies including barcodes, holograms, and optical markers are employed. Cutting-edge techniques, such as research on fluorescent inks employing both inorganic materials and organic dyes, are currently being explored. A promising solution lies in a distinct category of afterglow or persistent luminescent inorganic materials containing rare earth element dopants. Understanding the fundamental physics and tackling the challenges in developing appropriate afterglow phosphor materials and formulating security ink protocols could pave the way for innovative solutions and exciting technological advancements.

Fingerprints (FPs) unique characteristics make them pivotal evidence at crime scenes, aiding law enforcement in identifying and apprehending criminals [33,34]. Methods for detecting LFPs include ninhydrin, cyanoacrylate fumigation and the widely utilized powder dusting technique. In comparison to quantum dots, organic dyes, inorganic phosphors and metal powder, typically demonstrate enhanced stability and safety. They facilitate the visualization of LFPs with increased contrast, reduced background interference, and simpler in procedure of operation. As can be see from the table, most of the phosphors exhibit level I-II features [35]. However, the important level III features for personal identifications are not explored. Hence, it is imperative to explore fluorescent phosphors with high contrast, minimal background interference and capable of visualizing level I-III features of LFPs across level I to III for enhanced forensic detection. Table 1 [36-40] & Table 2 [41-45] shows the review of literature on rare earth doped phosphors used for fingerprint detection and anti-counterfeiting applications

This study involved incorporating CDs into ZAO:1Pr3+ to enhance the PL intensity of the phosphors. The surface morphology, crystallinity and thermal stability of the phosphor and composite are characterized and discussed in detail. Additionally, the optimized CDs@ZAO:Pr3+ NCs is utilized for LFPs, security inks and w-LEDs applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs (1–5 wt%) using urea and citric acid
ce:section.ce:para: A volume of 50 mL of deionized (DI) water is employed to dissolve 2 gm (equivalent to 1 mmol) of citric acid and 2 gm (equivalent to 3 mmol) of urea. Subsequently, the resulting solution is transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel reactor with a capacity of 150 mL and subjected to heating at 180 °C for a duration of 3 hrs. After the reactor had naturally cooled to room temperature, the solution underwent filtration using a microporous membrane (0.22 μm) to eliminate larger particles. Following this, ethanol precipitation is employed, followed by centrifugation. The re-dispersion or precipitation procedure is iterated three times to guarantee thorough elimination of precursor residues and minor derivatives. Subsequently, the samples are dried at 60 °C for 1 hr, resulting in the attainment of the final product, depicted as a blue solid (Fig. 1a ).

ZAO:Pr3+ (0.25–5 mol%) NPs are synthesized using alanine as a fuel through the solution combustion technique. The raw materials used in manufacturing both pure and 0.25–5 mol% Pr3+ doped ZAO are of analytical grade. These constituents comprised zinc nitrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O), aluminum nitrate (Al(NO3)3·9H2O), praseodymium nitrate (Pr(NO3)3·6H2O), and alanine (C3H7NO2). The precise amounts of precursors are dissolved in double-distilled water and vigorously agitated for approximately 20 min using a magnetic stirrer. An even and clear redox blend is achieved. Following this, the solution is introduced into the preheated muffle furnace, which is held at a temperature of 400 °C for 2 hrs. Within a short span, the solution commenced boiling, releasing a substantial quantity of gaseous by-products, leading to the creation of a notably voluminous ZAO:Pr3+sample (Fig. 1b).
The reaction involved in the formation of ZAO:Pr3+ is provided below: Zn(NO3)2 + 2Al(NO3)3 + 3.8C3H7NO2+Pr(NO3)3→ZnAl2O4Pr+7.5N2+13·53H2O+11.6CO2 (1)

Notably, the CDs are directly infused onto the surface of ZAO:Pr3+, eliminating the need for a dialysis step. The synthesized sample is then dried at 80 °C for 6 hrs in preparation for further analysis and applications. Moreover, the methodology allows for the straightforward and efficient production of large quantities of these NCs without necessitating an extensive procedure, as illustrated in Fig. 1c.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of security ink and creating AC pattern using 5Wt%CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs
ce:section.ce:para: A mixture consisting of 83% of DI water, di-ammonium phosphate (1% w/w; dispersant), and polyacrylic acid (15% w/w; stabilizer). The suspension is mechanically agitated for 60 min. Predetermined amounts of 5 wt% CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs are added to the solution, followed by mechanical agitation for 60 min and sonication (25 kHz) for 40 min to achieve a uniform dispersion. Using the security inks, AC patterns are created on the cardboard surface using the brush mode method to achieve the desired patterns. The surfaces are left to air dry up to 5 min before being captured under UV light at 365 nm. A schematic diagram depicting the brush mode method of AC patterns is shown in Fig. 1d.

Fig. 2 illustrates the primary stages of LFPs detection. LFPs are obtained from a 26-year-old female. Prior to touching various substrates, hands are thoroughly washed with soap, paying particular attention to the fingerprint lines. To create the LFPs, the procedure involved gently rubbing the forehead with a finger to transfer natural oils onto the skin surface, facilitating adhesion. The finger is then pressed onto a glass substrate. Subsequently, the experimenter delicately applied CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs onto the LFPs using a soft brush. The FPs are captured using a digital camera (Nikon Z8) illuminated by 365 nm UV light.

The tri-phosphors, comprising BaMgAl10O17:Eu2+, (Ba, Sr)2SiO4:Eu2+, and ZAO:1Pr3+ nanoparticles (NPs), along with CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ nanocrystals (NCs), are integrated into w-LED lamps. The luminous characteristics of these lamps are evaluated using the USB 4000 spectrometer from Ocean Optics.

Shimadzu 7000 powder X-ray diffractogram (XRD) (Cu Kα: λ = 1.5406 Å) with a scanning rate of 0.02°/min across a range from 10 to 60 ° is used to analyse the phase purity of each NPs. The Full Prof Suite program is utilized for Rietveld refinement to assess the structural parameters. The surface morphologies of NPs are analyzed using a scanning electron microscope (Hitachi-3000). Furthermore, the surface morphology of the NPs is explored using Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, JEOL-JEM-2100 PLUS, Japan). The chemical composition analysis of the constituent elements of the NPs is performed using the EDAX instrument (EDAX Apollo XT). Using a TEM (JEM-2010) instrument, TEM, HRTEM images and microscopic chemical composition can be acquired. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with a Vacuum Generator (VG) ESCALAB MKII spectrometer upgraded with an AlK source and an XR4 dual anode is used to analyse the binding energies of the samples. The PL properties are examined using a Horiba spectrofluorometer, with a 450 W Xenon lamp serving as the excitation source.

The XRD patterns of the Undoped and doped ZAO:Pr3+ (0.25–5 mol%), CDs and 5 wt% CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs are shown in Fig. 3(a) . XRD patterns acquired for all Pr3+ doped ZAO samples are indexed based on the standard data provided by the JCPDS card No: 05–0669 for the cubic ZAO spinel phase [46]. The peaks observed corresponded to diffraction lines indexed as (220), (311), (400), (331), (422), (333) and (440). As the concentration of Pr3+ increased, no additional diffraction peaks associated with impurities of Al2O3 and ZnO are detected in the patterns. Fig. 3(a) reveals that the (311) diffraction peak stands out as the most intense peak compared to the others. The XRD pattern of the CDs sample exhibits a broad peak centered at 2θ= 21.8° (2nd profile from top), corresponding to the carbon material. This confirms the single-phase nature of the prepared powder samples. Fig. 3(b) illustrates the structural refinement outcomes for the prepared sample ZAO:1Pr3+ NPs. These findings are consistent with both the calculated and observed values. The refinement process verified the presence of a pure cubic structure with Fd3m symmetry. The refined parameters indicate that a = b = c = 8.0869 nm, α = β = γ = 90°, and χ2 = 0.88. The quality of the structural refinement data is evaluated by calculating the goodness-of-fit parameter (GOF), which is defined as the ratio of Rwp to Rexp. Here, Rwp represents the weighted profile residual, while Rexp signifies the expected profile residual. The goodness-of-fit (GOF) value for the ZAO:1Pr3+ NPs sample is determined to be 1.13, indicating its proximity to unity [47]. Fig. 4 (a-e) showcases the Rietveld refinement of ZAO:Pr3+ (0.25–5 mol%) NPs. The Rietveld refinement parameters are utilized to generate the packing diagram using Diamond software. Fig. 4(F, g) depicts the cationic distributions in the tetrahedral and octahedral sites within the cubic unit cell of the ZAO:Pr3+ NPs spinel structures. The detailed crystal structural information is provided in Table 3 . The estimation of the average crystallite size (D) is conducted utilizing Scherrer's equation [48]:

Here, D represents the average crystallite size of the material, β indicates the full width at half maximum (FWHM) in radians, λ denotes the wavelength of X-rays, and θ represents Bragg's angle. The estimated D values are in the range 15–27 nm. The obtained values D and strain are presented in Table 4 . The full width at half maxima (FWHM) is expressed as a linear combination of micro-strain and crystallite size. The impact of micro-strain and crystallite size on FWHM is illustrated using Williamson–Hall (W–H) plots [49]. Eqn (7) illustrates that the W-H plot is constructed between 4sinθ and βcosθ (Fig. 3c). The strain is derived from the slope, while the crystallite size is derived from the intercept. The Size-Strain Plot (SSP) analysis employs the Lorentzian and Gaussian functions. Here, the Lorentzian function accounts for size broadening, while the Gaussian function represents strain broadening of the X-ray peak profile [50]. Thus, the total broadening in the SSP can be represented by the following equation:

In this equation, βL signifies the broadening of the peak due to the Lorentzian function, while βG indicates the broadening caused by the Gaussian function. The SSP method frequently yields improved results for isotropic expansion. Low-angle reflections, which provide higher precision and accuracy, are generally more complex than those at higher angles. Conversely, peaks at higher diffraction angles often exhibit considerable overlap, leading to lower-quality XRD data. As a result, the SSP measurement is performed using the following equation [51]:

The slope of the linear regression line provides the average size of the crystallites, while the intercept indicates the intrinsic strain of the nanoparticles.

In the SSP model, size broadening is represented by the Lorentz function, while strain broadening is represented by the Gaussian function, providing an explanation for X-ray peaks. However, the XRD peak does not conform precisely to either the Gaussian function or the Lorentz function. The Gaussian function effectively fits the peak region but does not precisely fit the tails, while the Lorentz function accurately match the tails but does not precisely fit the peak region [52,53]. To tackle this issue, Halder-Wagner (H-W) suggested that the broadening of the peak follows a symmetric Voigt function [54], which is a combination of the Gaussian and Lorentzian functions. As a result, the FWHM of the X-ray profile can be represented as follows:

This approach provides the benefit of assigning greater significance to the peaks in the low and mid angle ranges, where the overlap of diffracting peaks exhibits considerably less. FE-SEM technique is used to understand the morphology of ZAO:Pr3+ (0.25–1 mol%) NPs, CDs and 5wt%CDs@ ZAO:1Pr3+ composite (Fig. 5 ). The FE-SEM photographs of ZAO:Pr3+ (0.25–1 mol%) NPs revealed agglomeration-free hexagonal rod-like structures distributed across the surface. However, as the amount of Pr3+ ions in ZAO increases, the particle shape appears to remain consistent (Fig. 5a–f). Fig. 5g depicts a FE-SEM picture of CDs that clearly show spherical particles with well-defined edges. Fig. 5h depicts the FE-SEM picture of the 5wt%CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ composite, which clearly displays the CDs embedded on the phosphor. Fig. 5i-k depicts the average particle size determined using particle size distribution histograms. The histograms show an estimated particle size of ∼0.14 µm (ZAO:1Pr3+) and ∼4.25 nm (CDs). However, in the instance of 5wt%CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ composite, the average particle size for CDs is determined to be approximately 5.75 nm.

The microscopic characteristics of the CDs are determined by TEM inspection, as illustrated in Fig. 6a . The results show that the particles are spherical, homogeneously mono dispersed, and have no aggregation, measuring 6.13 nm in size. The estimated particle size ranges from 4 to 9 nm, and the particle size distribution histogram (Fig. 6b) shows a narrow size distribution of the CDs, which supports their size. The HR-TEM image (inset of Fig. 6g) revealed clear lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, which corresponds to the (100) of CD lattice planes and indicates strong crystallinity of the CDs. Fig. 6c depicts the TEM picture of ZAO:1Pr3+ NPs contain agglomerated particles measuring around 45–50 nm. Furthermore, the average particle size is estimated to be 43 nm, using the size distribution (Fig. 6d). Fig. 6h depicts an HR-TEM image of NPs, highlighting the (220) plane with an interplanar spacing of 0.24 nm. Fig. 6e shows the TEM image of the 5wt%CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ composite, with the average particle size of the CDs in the composite measured at 6.15 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 6f. Fig. 6i depicts the HR-TEM image of the 5wt%CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ composite, with the average inter-planar distances found to be 0.22 nm for the (100) plane of the CDs and 0.24 nm for the (220) plane of the ZAO:1Pr3+ NPs. Significantly, the inter-planar d-spacing obtained from HR-TEM images closely corresponds to the GATAN software results. CDs have a strong affinity for water due to the vast amount of carboxylic acid components present on their surface. They are well suited for further integration with other polymeric, organic, inorganic, or biologically active materials. The TEM image of 5wt%CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ composite shows tight adherence of the individual CDs to the surface of ZAO:1Pr3+, which is clearly explained by the binding between carboxylic acid components and ZAO:1Pr3+, allowing the boundaries between CDs and the ZAO:1Pr3+ surface to be distinguished.

To ascertain the purity of element composition and element homogeneity, EDAX spectra of a specified area of ZAO:1Pr3+ NPs and 5wt%CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs is shown in Fig. 7a & f. SEM–EDAX mapping analysis is performed to assess the chemical uniformity of the NPs (inset of Fig. 7(a & f)). The elemental mapping indicated that all the elements are homogeneously distributed in the host lattice. This homogeneous distribution of the Pr3+ ions in the ZAO host lattice and 5wt%CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ composite is expected to deliver better luminescence properties (Fig. 7b-e and g-k). The inset table of Fig. 7a & f shows the wt.% distribution of elements used in the prepared phosphor. The chemical constituents of the sample and the incorporation of the Pr3+ ions in the ZAO host and 5wt%CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ composite are confirmed from the EDAX analysis and it demonstrated the absence of any other impurity.

XPS analysis is utilized to determine the composition and chemical state of Pr3+ doped ZAO NPs. Fig. 8a illustrates the XPS spectrum of ZAO:1Pr3+ nanoparticles, confirming the presence of Zn, Al, O, Pr, and C in the phosphors. In ZAO phosphors, the Zn 2p spectra mainly comprise two distinct peaks cantered at 1021.6 and 1044.7 eV (Fig. 8b), corresponding to the Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 binding energies of ZAO, respectively. The XPS spectra of Al 2p in ZAO:1Pr3+ NPs exhibits a well-fitted Gaussian peak at 73.80 eV, as depicted in Fig. 8c. The peak observed at 73.80 eV, indicative of a higher binding energy, is attributed to octahedral Al. In Fig. 8d, the XPS spectra of O 1 s in ZAO:1Pr3+ nanoparticles are depicted. The spectrum exhibits three Gaussian peaks at 529.75, 530.73, and 531.76 eV, which are attributed to the M-O bonds (Zn–O and Al–O) within the ZAO crystal lattice. In the present nanoparticles, the peak with lower binding energy corresponds to Zn–O bonds in tetrahedral sites of ZAO NPs, whereas the peak with higher binding energy is associated with the Al–O bonds in octahedral sites of ZAO NPs [55]. Fig. 8e displays the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s of CDs. The C 1 s spectra can be deconvoluted into three peaks representing different carbon species: sp2 C (C = C/CC) at 284.20 eV, sp3 C (CO and CN) at 285.79 eV, and -C = O at 287.34 eV. The Pr 3d spectra in ZAO NPs predominantly exhibit two peaks positined at 933.66 and 954.08 eV (Fig. 8f), corresponding to Pr 3d3/2 and Pr 3d5/2, respectively [56].

The PLE spectra for the ZAO with varying Pr3+ concentrations with maintaining the emission wavelength at 614 nm is illustrates in Fig. 9a . Three neighbouring bands are detected at 447, 470 and 483 nm, related to the intra f-f transitions 3H4→3Pj (j = 2,1,0) respectively [57,58]. The peak with the highest intensity observed at 447 nm suggests that the excitation predominantly populates the 3P2 level. Thus, we expect to observe a prevailing PL spectra for the series of ZAO:Pr3+ (0.25–5 mol%) NPs when excited at 447 nm. Fig. 9b illustrates the PL spectra of the synthesized phosphors under excitation at 447 nm. Several emission bands at 573,598, 614, 653, 675, 693, 712 and 735 nm corresponding to the 3P0→3H5, 1D2→3H4, 3P0→3H6, 3P0→3F2, 3P1→3F3, 3P0→3F3, 3P1→3F4 and 3P0→3F4 transitions can be distinguished [59]. Two notable radiative de-excitations occur from the 3P0 multiplet to 3H6 and 3F2, leading to the bands of emission in the orange red regions, respectively. The emission spectrum confirms that the NPs is a promising reddish-orange phosphor. The bands centered at 614 and 653 nm are significantly more enhanced for the Pr3+ ion [60]. Fig. 9c shows the cumulative emission intensity shows a significant rise with the concentration doping until the critical value reaching up to 1 mol%. subsequently, it decreases with additional increases in Pr3+ concentration. This phenomenon is linked to cross-relaxation (CR), where the exchange interaction becomes active when the interatomic distance between two nearest Pr3+ ions is sufficiently close (≤ 5 Å). Energy transfer depends on the overlap of the orbital wave functions of the relevant centers. Thus, the closer the Pr3+→Pr3+ distance, the greater the energy exchange between the pair. The critical distance (Rc) can be estimated using Blasse's expression [61]:
(12)
R c
= 2
(
3 V
4 π C N
)
1 / 3

In this context, V represents the volume of the unit cell, C signifies the critical concentration of doping ions, and N stands for the number of host cations in the unit cell. For ZAO, with V = 528.86 Å³ and N = 5, the critical concentration of Pr3+ in the ZAO host is 0.01. Thus, the critical distance (Rc) of Pr3+ ions is calculated to be 27.23 Å. The energy transfer between Pr3+ ions occurs as a result of electric multipolar interaction, which can be quantified using the equation:
(13)
1 x
= k
[
1 + β
( X )
Q / 3
]
− 1

In this equation, X denotes the concentration of Pr3+ions, while k and β are constants. Q assumes values of 3, 6, 8, and 10 for dipole–dipole (d-d), dipole–quadrupole (d-q), and quadrupole–quadrupole (q-q) interactions. The value of Q is determined by plotting log(X) against log(I/X) (Fig. 9d), resulting in a linear graph with a slope of −1.13. The computed Q value is determined to be 3.39, closely resembling 3. This indicates that concentration quenching in ZAO primarily arises from dipole–dipole interaction [62].

The PL spectra of the obtained CDs are presented in Fig. 9e. The excitation spectrum exhibits a broad band with a peak at 446 nm, while the emission spectrum comprises a broad band with a peak at 501 nm. These are typical luminescence characteristics of CDs, with the broad excitation and emission spectra primarily attributed to the surface states of CDs [63-65]. The emission intensity of CDs@ZAO:Pr3+ (1–5 wt%) NCs is illustrated in Fig. 9f. Specifically, the emission intensity of 5 wt% CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs increased by 39.02 folds compared to that of ZAO:1Pr3+ NPs. The excitation energy can be transferred more effectively from CDs to ZAO:Pr3+(Fig. 9 g).

The possibility of Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) between CDs and Pr3+ ions is one explanation for this phenomenon. In theory, FRET needs three basic requirements: The donor and acceptor molecules must, first and foremost, be near to one another (usually within 1–10 nm). This need is supported by the TEM image of the CDs@ ZAO:1Pr3+ composite (Fig. 6), which shows the CDs firmly attached to the ZAO:Pr3+ surface. Second, the acceptors (ZAO:Pr3+) excitation spectrum and the donor's (CDs) emission spectrum ought to overlap. This energy transfer can be observed in the spectral region shown in Fig. 9h. Effective energy transfer is facilitated by the large spectral overlap that can be achieved between the PL of CDs and the PLE of phosphors thanks to the broad PL spectra of CDs [66–69]. A higher spectrum overlap is seen in the CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+composite, which lends more credence to the possibility of FRET. As a result, ZAO:Pr3+can receive the excitation energy from CDs with greater effectiveness. The donor and acceptor transition dipole orientations also normally have to be parallel [70,71]. The transition dipole moments of donor and acceptors interact d-d to enhance energy transfer, which is made possible by FRET. Once the donor (CDs) absorbs light and becomes excited, it can either radiatively relax through fluorescence or non-radiatively transfer energy to the acceptor (Pr3+). The electrons in Pr3+ transition from the 3P0 to different components of the excited 3f energy level when CDs@ZAO:Pr3+ are excited at a wavelength of 447 nm, which describes the mechanism of luminescence enhancement. On the other hand, electrons in CDs transition, with the help of absorbed photon energy, from the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) to the Lowest Un-occupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) (Fig. 9i). When compared to ZAO:Pr3+ phosphors, the emission intensity of 5wt% CDs@ZAO:Pr3+ composite shows an increase of about 39.02 times (Fig. 9f). The shift in FWHM values points to a reduction in positive and negative defects as a result of CDs inclusion, producing an oxidation/reduction effect, which is responsible for the increased crystallinity in the 5wt% CDs@ZAO:Pr3+ composite. Additionally, the 5wt% CDs@ZAO:Pr3+ composite show a wide 400–500 nm excitation spectrum with a major peak at 447 nm, which corresponds to the emission range (425–600 nm) of common LED chips. This property increases the range of possible uses for luminous materials while providing improved performance and adaptability [102]. A comparative analysis of improved PL in various CDs comprised from different research is shown in Table 5 [72–77].

The CP of the optimized samples is computed and found to be 94.7%. The findings above suggest that the current phosphor holds promise for LED applications. Table 6 compiles the values for CIE coordinates, CCT, and CP for ZAO:Pr3+ (0.25–5 mol%) NPs and CDs@ZAO:Pr3+ (1–5 wt%) NCs. As a result, the prepared NPs can offer advantages for applications such as Ultra Violet-Light Emitting Diodes (UV-LEDs). The values for CIE coordinates, CCT, and CP for the ZAO:Pr3+ (0.25–5 mol 5) NPs and CDs@ZAO:Pr3+ (1–5 wt%) NCs are tabulated in Table 6.

To investigate the influence of temperature, PL spectra of ZAO:1Pr3+ NPs and 5 wt% CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs are measured at numerous temperatures ranging from 303 to 463 K, is depicted in Fig. 10 (b, d). It is apparent that the PL intensity gradually decreases with increase in temperature, while the positions of the emission bands remains unchanged. The contour plot offers a clearer visualization of the relationship between temperature and emission spectra. In Fig. 10(a, c), the temperature ranges from 303 to 463 K. Comparing the luminescence intensity of optimized NPs and NCs at 423 K, we observe values of 92.4% and 95.4%, respectively. This performance surpasses that of commercial red-light phosphors like K5La(MoO4):Eu3+ (74.3%), as illustrated in Fig. 10e. To elucidate the thermal quenching mechanism of ZAO:1Pr3+ NPs and 5 wt% CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs, the activation energy (Ea) could be determined using the Arrhenius formula [80]:

Substituting the relevant values into the Eqn, Ea is determined to be 0.16 and 0.21 eV for ZAO:1Pr3+ NPs and 5 wt% CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs, as shown in Fig. 10f. Sensitivities are calculated at various temperatures and depicted in Fig. 10j. It is observed that sensitivities decreased with rising temperature. The highest sensitivity recorded is 25.1 × 10−4 and 75.48×10−4 K −1 at 303 K. In this section, Table 7 presents a summary of sensitivity values for various host materials doped with Pr3+ as reported in previous studies [84-88].

To explore the luminescence performance and assess their applicability for solid-state lighting, it is crucial to ascertain the quantum efficiency of the phosphor. This efficacy, which is defined as the ratio of emitted photons to absorbed photons, can be represented by the following formula [89]:
(23)

∫

− ∫
E s

LS refers to the emission spectrum of ZAO:1Pr3+ nanoparticles and 5 wt% CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ nanocrystals, where η denotes the IQE value. ES and ER denote the excitation line spectra consisting solely of the phosphor and BaSO4 reference, respectively. The IQE of ZAO:1Pr3+ NPs achieves 72.9%, while 5 wt% CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs achieves 84% at 447 nm (Fig. 10k& l). These results highlight the potential of 5 wt% CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs as a promising material for white LEDs.

The lifetime decay curve for both ZAO:1Pr3+ NPs and 5 wt% CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs is illustrated in Fig. 11a & b, at excitation wavelength λexci = 447 nm and emission wavelength λemi = 614 nm. All the lifetime curves exhibit initial exponential decay, characterized by the equation [90,91]:
(24)
I =
I 0
e
− t
τ

Here, I and IO represent the luminescence intensities at time t and 0, respectively. The initial exponential decay of the curve suggests a homogeneous distribution of dopant ions within the host matrix. Consequently, the lifetime values are determined to be 3.07 ms for ZAO:1Pr3+ NPs and 3.75 ms for 5 wt% CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs.

Furthermore, the IQE and absorption of ZAO:1Pr3+ NPs are assessed to be 72.9%, respectively, showcasing exceptional luminescence performance. Additionally, the incorporation of CDs enhances the IQE of ZAO:1Pr3+ NPs, increasing it from 72.9% to 84%. Given that QE serves as a crucial metric for assessing the performance of phosphors in applications, prototype WLEDs devices have been constructed to evaluate the practical utility of the synthesized ZAO:1Pr3+ NPs and 5 wt% CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs. These devices integrate a 395 nm InGaN chip along with green (Sr, Ba)2SiO4:Eu2+ and cyan BAM:Eu2+ NPs, along with ZAO:1Pr3+ NPs and 5 wt% CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs. The electroluminescence spectra (EL) of both WLEDs devices closely match their respective PL spectra, providing confirmation of stable luminescence [92] (Fig. 11c& d). At a current drive of 20 mA, w-LED-1 exhibits a colour rendering index (Ra) of 79.8, luminous efficacy of 9.6 lm/W, and a corrected CCT of 4667 K. Meanwhile, w-LED-2 demonstrates an Ra of 85.8, luminous efficacy of 9.1 lm/W, and a CCT of 4224 K. Moreover, the CIE coordinate positions of the fabricated w-LED devices are located at (0.3687, 0.3610) for w-LED-1 and (0.3514, 0.3756) for w-LED-2. Additionally, stability with respect to current is another crucial criterion for assessing the properties of w-LEDs.

Fingerprint identification technology holds significant importance in forensic science due to the unique and unchanging nature of FPs, which differ for each individual and each finger, regardless of age. The optimized CDs@ZAO:Pr3+ NCs is widely acknowledged as one of the most efficient materials for visualizing LFPs. Its ability to unveil high-contrast ridge patterns through a straightforward, efficient, and non-destructive powder dusting method enhances its usefulness in forensic investigations [102,103]. The process of visualizing LFPs using the powder dusting method entails applying the composite onto the area where the latent fingerprint impression is located. This application allows the composite to adhere to the residual sweat components left behind by the friction ridge skin on the fingers. In this context, the selection of the optimized phosphor is deliberate, with a specific focus on latent fingerprint visualization. The choice is based on the superior characteristics of CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+NCs, which offer enhanced CP and heightened sensitivity to the naked eye. Moreover, the composite size is smaller than a μm, underscoring its potential as a promising material for detecting LFPs [104]. This is due to its effectiveness in highlighting essential recognition features of FPs, such as ridges and hooks. Fig. 13a illustrates both bright field optical images captured under daylight and fluorescence images taken under 365 nm UV light of LFPs developed on various substrates, including paper, aluminum foil, and metal. It is apparent from the images that all the FPs display unique ridge flow and pattern configurations, with clear resolution discernible to the naked eye. Analysis of the grayscale pixel profiles of the highlighted white line on the developed FPs reveals that the prepared composite is precisely deposited on the ridges rather than in the furrows [105]. This accurate deposition is attributed to the nano-scale dimensions of the composite particles and their enhanced adhesive properties (Fig. 13b). This observation confirms earlier findings, where the pixel values are observed to be higher in the ridge areas and lower in the furrow regions. Additionally, 3D plots of the developed FPs provide further evidence, demonstrating the uniform distribution of the stained composites across the surface of the LFPs (Fig. 13c).

Fig. 13d presents both the local and total fluorescent images of FPs on a glass substrate. The skin of the fingers contains numerous oil and sweat glands, facilitating the deposition of fingerprint traces. During various activities, sweat and oils are continuously secreted from these glands. Hence, when fingers come into contact with surfaces, they naturally leave behind marks. These FPs consist of a variety of components, including inorganic salts such as chlorides, phosphates and organic substances such as amino acids and fats as well as water. The preferred adhesive material in this chemical composition comprises a blend of CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs. Fig. 13d clearly illustrates the ridge patterns, showcasing intricate details such as the core, enclosure, hook, crossover, eye, bridge, dot, island, ridge end and bifurcation. Fingerprint classification serves to streamline searching and matching processes. Materials like the tetraphenylethene (TPE) based dye [106] and cationic dye-diatomite composites [107] previously used for latent fingerprint visualization fell short in clearly and comprehensively presenting the overall fingerprint image, especially in capturing the intricate details of level III features. Level I characteristics encompass the distinct features found in one or more core areas of a fingerprint, often referred to as private or exclusive areas. Examples of level I characteristics include the delta and loop patterns observed in the fingerprint. Level II characteristics, which are widely employed in fingerprint matching, consist of identifiable components such as bifurcations and ridge endings. Furthermore, attributes such as pores and scars within the overall ridge structure, classified as level III characteristics, are crucial for achieving precise matches. Among these, pores play a vital role in individual identification. The composition of a person's sweat remains relatively consistent over time, ensuring the stability and uniqueness of sweat characteristics for fingerprinting purposes. Consequently, sweat traits serve as reliable markers for personal identification. It is evident that FPs treated with CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs exhibit distinguishable features spanning from level I-III characteristics.

Effect of temperature on storage of latent fingerprints examined with optimized 5wt% CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs. The effect of temperature on the storage of the latent fingerprints is investigated on marble substrate with this fluorescent material [108]. The storage is taken in various temperature for 20 min in the hot temperature furnace. The resulting FPs are captured using a camera and depicted in Fig. 14 . The study is conducted at temperature of 0, 50, 100 and 150 °C. It is found that the temperature affected on the fluorescence of the dust powder. The developed fingerprint is not affected by temperature even up to 100 °C of treatment. When the fingerprint is stored at higher temperatures (150 °C), it will slightly decrease the glow of the latent fingerprint which is caused by the decay of optimized CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs if the temperature increases (Fig. 14a). Even at 150 °C, although a minimal decrease in the fluorescence intensity is observed, the ridge features remained easily discernible. Fig. 14b shows the pixel profiles of the selected regions on the thermally treated fingerprints. It is clearly observed from the respective grayscale plots that, the fingerprints treated with 0, 50 and 100 °C showed a high contrast image, higher pixel value, better finger ridge details and a clear distinction between ridges and furrows of the fingerprint compared the FPs treated for 150 °C temperature. As can be seen from the images and pixel profiles, the prepared optimized NC is stable up to 100 °C. Further, in order to test the sensitivity of the NCs, 3D interactive plots are generated using ImageJ software [109]. As can be seen from the 3D plots, the optimized NCs stacked clearly on the ridges rather than the furrows (Fig. 14c). Therefore, the NCs is potential candidate for the development of FPs. In conclusion, CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs show promising applications in LFP detection, paving the way for further advancements in forensic area.

The YOLOv8x (You Only Look Once version 8) deep learning technique is currently gaining popularity as a real-time object detector due to its accuracy, speed, and resilience. As a result, we can do quick detection (>60 frames per second (FPS)) with the YOLO detector, ensuring real-time detection. Furthermore, as comparison with several deep-CNN detectors, YOLO has a considerably lower demand for computer hardware. So, in the present work, we applied YOLOv8x technique in the detection of FP features, which is most recent version of YOLO detector. To increase the detected performance on FP features, we first create a specialized dataset including various types of FPs developed by CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs, which is annotated with and bounding boxes and then we upgrade the YOLOv8x network to identify minutiae objects such as ridge end and bifurcation. During the training process, the network is fed a large number of fingerprint images and associated annotations, which allows it to learn the patterns as well as the features of fingerprint minutiae. The sigmoid activation and ReLU functions are used to train the YOLOv8x models over a 39-epoch period. A V100 server equipped with an Intel(R) Core(TM) i5–3470 CPU and a 32 GB RAM is used to implement the deep learning models. The YOLOv8x model can identify and localize minute characteristics in unseen fingerprint images once it has been trained. Convolutional and pooling layers are applied by the algorithm to extract features from an input fingerprint image. Next, employing bounding boxes, these characteristics are leveraged to forecast the presence and positions of minutiae points. The outcome of the YOLOv8x algorithm for fingerprint recognition is a collection of bounding boxes encompassing the detected minutiae points identified as the output of the fingerprint recognition algorithm YOLOv8x. These bounding boxes offer the spatial coordinates of the minutiae, enabling further processing and analysis, such as matching and verification with a database of known FPs. To ensure the effectiveness of automated fingerprint recognition systems across various applications like biometric identification, access control, and law enforcement, YOLOv8x offers a rapid and accurate approach for detecting and pinpointing minute details in fingerprint images. Fig. 15a illustrates the schematic representation of the computational analysis process for fingerprint identification employed in this study. The YOLOv8x object detection analysis process comprises several steps as outlined below.
1.
Pre-processing: Original input fingerprint images using directly in deep learning for extraction of traits will knowingly reduce experimental accuracy, so fingerprint images must be pre-processed. The input fingerprint images must undergo pre-processing. For compatibility with the YOLOv8x model, pre-processing steps may encompass resizing, normalization, annotations, binarization, and various other transformations. The main objective function is the auto annotation function used to evaluate the performance of the deep-algorithm. Essentially, we aim to reduce the error when using deep neural networks. In some instances, the value derived from the loss function is referred to simply as "loss," and the objective function is often associated with a loss function [110]. After the input fingerprint image has been pre-processed, and it has execute with YOLOv8x model. The fingerprint image undergoes propagation through the network layers, extracting features at various scales, and predicting bounding boxes, confidence scores, and class probabilities for potential objects in the image.
2.
Training Model: In the training model, four pre-processed fingerprint images are used with pre-trained weights which will be trained on YOLOv8x model (Fig. 15b). Those fingerprint images have the same particulars, such as the Image size ratio, object size, but they differ in illumination because they are created using CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs. The model comprises a deep learning neural network, typically based on convolutional neural networks (CNNs), which proves particularly effective for recognizing features in fingerprint images.

The FPs are not ideal to directly apply for the feature detection in YOLOv8x modal, because characteristics in the fingerprint images are usually overlapped, densely distributed, very small in size and often overlapped, large in number. To address these issues, this paper modifies the YOLOv8x original network structure in three ways: A 8-fold down sampling small fusion of features layer is added in place of the 32-fold down-sampling large-size feature fusion layer. After the network has been pruned, move the FPN and PAN structures there and choose the proper SPP pooling kernel parameters. A kernel is applied to the input image in the convolutional layer of YOLOv8x in order to decrease its size by a factor of 8. The convolutional layer produces a 13×13 activation map from a 416×416 input image. To ensure an odd number of activation map locations, we modify the YOLOv8x framework by resizing the input image to dimensions like 608×608 or any other value that is a multiple of 32 [18, 19, Deep Learning-based approach for detection and classification of Micro/Mini drones]. The FPs are formatted as 608×608 JPG images during the training and testing phases of this experiment. For the training process, 39 epochs are used, with a batch size of 32. The precision, recall, and F1 score curves are used to assess our detection model. The curves are generated by adjusting the detection threshold. Consequently, precision, recall, and F1 score serve as crucial model evaluation metrics, all of which indicate the accuracy of the detector. As a result, each of these terms has a deeper and distinct meaning. The mAP50, mAP50–95, and validation/box loss curves are also used as performance evaluation metrics for the YOLOv8x model. The mean average precision across all classes is represented by the mAP score [111,112]. It is significant to note that the mAP score for the classification of multiple labels is distinct from the evaluation of models for object detection because Intersection over Union (IoU) is used as the detection threshold. The overlap between predicted and ground truth boxes is measured by the IoU metric. We set the IoU threshold to 0.5 for object detection precision and mAP score determination.

The Precision: Precision refers to the percentage of our analysis results.

Mean Average Precision (
m A P
): mAP is a statistical metric used to assess the effectiveness of computer vision models. It gives an aggregated evaluation of numerous iterations of the same model while taking precision and recall into account. mAP provides a single comprehensive metric by calculating the average of the Average Precision values across all classes in a model. The mAP metric has a value between 0 and 1, where a higher value indicating the capacity of a model to keep a low false negative and false positive rate. A larger mAP magnitude correlates with the models precision and recall, improving its performance.

FP stands for false positives, P stands for precision, TP stands for true positives, FN stands for false negatives, AP stands for average precision and R stands for recall.

Figs. 16a -l shows the results with 39 and 49 epochs. The results are shown in Figs. 16a-l for various epochs, enabling a comparison of the modified method's performance with that of the baseline approach (the original YOLOv8x). Fingerprint identification techniques perform better when precision and recall curves approach one. Specifically, with our YOLOv8x model, we observe from the curves that precision, recall, and mAP can reach a value close to approximately 0.7 in the interim. It is obvious that using the improved method will result in better detection performance. Figs. 16e-F and k-l show the improved FP-YOLO base model's box loss (box_loss), classification loss (cls_loss), and distribution focal loss (dfl_loss) after 39 and 49 rounds of training, respectively. The Figure also shows the detection results of the YOLOv8x model on the validation set. Furthermore, the enhanced model exhibits more comprehensive features, reduced rates of missed and false detections, and increased confidence in the detection outcomes. The model's response is shown at different confidence levels in Figs. 17a , c-d and e, g-h, which include precision, recall, and F1 curves. A confidence level of 1 is used to determine the ideal model performance. Furthermore, using the YOLOv8x model and the Precision, Recall, and F1 curves, Fig. 17b and F show the PR (precision-recall) curve for the ridge end and bifurcation dataset.

3. Prediction: In order to refine the bounding box coordinates and eliminate low-confidence detections, the class probabilities, predicted bounding boxes, and confidence scores undergo post-processing, as illustrated in Fig. 15c. Often, overlapping bounding boxes are removed and the most reliable detections are chosen using non-maximum suppression (NMS). The YOLOv8x model analysis produces a list of detected fingerprint features, as well as the associated bounding boxes and class labels, as the final output. These results can subsequently undergo processing, visualization, or utilization for follow-up tasks such as object tracking, recognition, or classification.

Fig. 18a & b depicts the FPs revealed by CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs on a glass surface, captured under 365 nm UV light. Leveraging fingerprint images directly with deep learning for dimensionality reduction and feature extraction can significantly enhance experimental accuracy. The existing fingerprint preparation technique is nearly optimal. To address this, we propose a ground-breaking YOLOv8x embedded deep learning model capable of instantaneous feature recognition. YOLO utilizes the entire fingerprint as a single entity to predict the bounding box coordinates. [113]. For every bounding box, class probabilities are calculated, and YOLO can process more than 60 frames per second. We employ a deep convolutional neural network (DCNN) to make precise predictions regarding the features [114]. Our deep YOLOv8x deep learning model detects bifurcation and ridge end features, as shown in Fig. 16a. We trained the model over 39 epochs for bifurcation and 49 epochs for ridge end using labelled input FPs. Each region consists of a 7 × 7 grid capable of predicting five bounding boxes. The proposed model effectively detects both bifurcation and ridge end features. The pre-processing in this study incorporates binarization, as illustrated in Fig. 18c& d. Bounding boxes are determined based on the confidence score, which is derived from the conditional probability of locating the target object. The proposed YOLOv8x model simultaneously identifies 7 × 7 cell grid cells and predicts a bounding box's confidence score of 0.3 using logistic regression. The results showcase the speed and efficiency of the model.

In summary, the visualization of FPs captured under 365 nm UV light underscores the significance of employing deep learning techniques for dimensionality reduction and feature extraction, thereby enhancing experimental accuracy. Further, YOLOv8x model shows good accuracy for the fingerprint developed by CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs and it offers a more precise and high-contrast image under 365 nm UV exposure compared to other solutions. Although the current fingerprint preparation technique is highly efficient, there is a need for a revolutionary real-time feature identification approach. Our proposed YOLOv8x deep learning embedded model offers a solution by accurately predicting bounding box coordinates and class probabilities while operating at high speeds. Furthermore, our deep convolutional neural network (DCNN) accurately predicts bifurcation and ridge end features. Through extensive training and pre-processing methods like binarization, our model effectively detects faults and exhibits efficient performance. The precision and the dependence on training boxes results validate our model's capability to identify bifurcation and ridge end features in FPs. Overall, this study presents a robust and efficient approach to fingerprint analysis, paving the way for enhanced identification and applications in forensic science.

This study reports the successful synthesis of orange-red-emitting ZAO:1Pr3+ NPs using the combustion route and CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs via the hydrothermal technique. By integrating CDs with ZAO:1Pr3+ NPs to create doped CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs, we have devised a simple approach to enhance the luminescence properties of these nanoparticles. Despite the fact that the emission intensity of 5 wt% CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+NCs increased by a factor of 39.02, the mechanism for this enhancement could be attributed to the energy transfer between CDs and the luminescent Pr3+. In optical thermometry or temperature sensing applications, the 5 wt% CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs exhibited impressive sensitivity values (Sr) of 44.51×10−4 K −1 and 75.48×10−4 K −1 for emission intensities at 673, 693, 712 and 735 nm, respectively. The lifetime values are determined to be 3.07 ms for ZAO:1Pr3+ NPs and 3.75 ms for 5 wt% CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs. Moreover, the powder dusting technique enables the visualization of LFPs across different substrates, unveiling level III intricacies such as fingerprint ridge width and sweat pore attributes. Even in cases of partial LFPs, identity verification remains achievable through meticulous level III analysis, offering dependable evidence for recognition purposes. YOLOv8x deep learning embedded model offers a solution by accurately predicting bounding box coordinates and class probabilities, all achieved with remarkable processing speeds. Furthermore, our deep convolutional neural network (DCNN) consistently predicts bifurcation and ridge end features. With thorough training and pre-processing techniques such as binarization, our model adeptly identifies faults and demonstrates efficient performance. Moreover, these nanocrystals are preferred for enhancing AC measures in applications related to data security. Also, warm w-LEDs are constructed using the red-emitting ZAO:1Pr3+NPs and 5 wt% CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs, showcasing exceptional EL performance with high CRI values (79.8 and 85.8) and low CCT values (4229 and 4864 K). This indicates that the 5 wt% CDs@ZAO:1Pr3+ NCs is an excellent candidate for the red-component in high-quality w-LED devices. Finally, the optimized composite opens up possibilities for diverse applications, including w-LEDs, thermal sensing, LFPs identification and AC measures applications.
